title: Beyond conventional skyrmions in synthetic antiferromagnets
authors: Kayla Fallon, Reshma Peremadathil-Pradeep, Christopher E. A. Barker, Zoey Tumbleson, Emily Darwin, Andrea Meo, Eloi Haltz, Benjamin A. Brereton, Trevor Almeida, Colin Kirkbride, Sara Villa, Sophie A. Morley, Mario Carpentieri, Riccardo Tomasello, Hans J. Hug, Christopher H. Marrows, Stephen McVitie
abstract: Magnetic skyrmions are topologically protected spin textures that can act as reconfigurable nanoscale information carriers. In synthetic antiferromagnets (SAFs), interlayer exchange coupling offers an additional control parameter beyond the interfacial Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction (DMI) and magnetic anisotropy. Here, we engineer a SAF composed of two chemically distinct ferromagnets (CoB and CoFeB), in which the external magnetic field and interlayer exchange act asymmetrically on the sublattices. The competition of these effects, acting as a resultant effective-field, gives rise to two distinct skyrmion families in different field regimes. In large fields, conventional-polarity skyrmions nucleate, with core antiparallel to the external field, whereas in smaller fields an inverse-polarity skyrmion state emerges as the effective-field reverses sign and almost saturates the CoFeB layers. Return-point memory measurements confirm independent nucleation pathways for the two families. Using element-resolved x-ray magnetometry, correlative magnetic force and Lorentz transmission electron microscopies, and parameter-matched micromagnetic modelling, we show that all textures reside only in the CoFeB layers, which experience a Ruderman-Kittel-Kasuya-Yosida (RKKY) exchange field originating from the CoB layers. This effective-field method provides a robust route to programmable three-dimensional spin textures with controlled polarity in selected layers of a multilayer with potential for applications in skyrmion-based computing and spin-logic architectures.
summary: This paper describes the engineering of a synthetic antiferromagnet that hosts two different types of skyrmions with opposite polarities. By using chemically distinct layers, the researchers created a system where an effective field can be tuned to control spin textures in a specific layer. This approach allows for the creation of programmable 3D spin architectures. Such advancements are significant for the development of next-generation skyrmion-based computing and spin-logic devices.
main_problem: The study investigates how to engineer programmable 3D spin textures in synthetic antiferromagnets (SAFs) by using chemical asymmetry and competing magnetic interactions to move beyond conventional skyrmion types.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated the emergence of two distinct skyrmion families (conventional and inverse polarity) localized exclusively in the CoFeB layer, driven by an effective field resulting from the competition between external fields and RKKY exchange.
method: The work combines experimental techniques including SQUID-VSM, MFM, Lorentz TEM, and element-resolved XMCD with quantitative micromagnetic simulations using the PETASPIN solver.
model_or_system: A compensated synthetic antiferromagnet (SAF) multilayer stack consisting of alternating CoB and CoFeB sublattices, engineered with asymmetric magnetic energy scales.
key_observables: Skyrmion polarity, magnetic induction, magnetization loops, return-point memory values, and element-specific magnetization.
important_parameters: Interlayer exchange coupling (RKKY), Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction (DMI), perpendicular magnetic anisotropy (PMA), and external magnetic field strength.
main_assumptions: Micromagnetic simulations were conducted at zero temperature, and the interfacial DMI was treated as a free parameter.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the evolution of magnetic textures; Figure 2 presents correlative imaging (MFM, SAXS, Lorentz STEM); Figure 3 displays element-resolved XMCD loops; Figure 4 shows micromagnetic simulation snapshots; Figure 5 illustrates return-point memory nucleation paths.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the experimental foundation (structural and magnetic parameters), moves to macroscopic magnetization measurements, progresses to microscopic element-specific imaging, and concludes with micromagnetic validation of the observed textures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: SAF multilayer and compensated m(H) response. a, Schematic of a single repeat of the multilayer, Ta(50)/[Ru(6)/Pt(9)/CoFeB(7)/Ru(6)/Pt(9)/CoB(9)]×10/Ru(6)/Pt(20); numbers are rounded actual layer thicknesses in ˚A as determined from XRR, given in the methods section. b, Cross-section ADF STEM image showing well-defined interfaces and high structural uniformity across repeats. c, Out-of-plane SQUID-VSM loop displaying a near-zero moment plateau around zero field (approximately ±30 mT), consistent with a fully compensated SAF.

Figure 2: Correlative MFM, resonant SAXS and Lorentz STEM DPC imaging of the two skyrmion families. Top row (MFM, up-magnetised tip): a, uniformly magnetised FM state at +150 mT; b, conventional-polarity skyrmions with cores antiparallel to the external field (bright) at +120 mT; c, maze-domains at +80 mT; d, inverse-polarity skyrmions with cores parallel to the external field (dark) at +45 mT; e, laterally uniform SAF state at 0 mT. Middle row, f,-j,: SAXS patterns at fields matched to MFM data. Bottom row (Lorentz DPC STEM at matched states, fixed tilt 17◦, and line profile): k, conventional-polarity skyrmions at −131 mT; l, maze-domains at −89 mT; m, inverse-polarity skyrmions at −49 mT;...

Figure 3: Element-resolved XMCD out-of-plane loops and sublattice decomposition. Transmission intensity I(H) is linearly related to m(H) for the selected element. a, Co-tuned loop ICo(H) (combined CoB+CoFeB response); b, Fe-tuned loop IFe(H) (CoFeB-only); c, recon- structed net loop Itotal(H) = IFe(H) + [ICo(H) −aIFe(H)] (purple), which reproduces the zero- moment plateau around µ0H = ±30 mT observed by SQUID-VSM (green); d, isolated CoB signal ICoB(H) = ICo(H) −aIFe(H), which is a fully square loop. The diode current has been inverted for comparison to SQUID-VSM data. The scaling factor a was determined as described in Methods.

Figure 4: Micromagnetic simulations results. a, conventional-polarity skyrmions at −130 mT; b, maze-domains at −80 mT; c, inverse-polarity skyrmions at −50 mT; d, uniform SAF state at 0 mT. Textures form exclusively in the CoFeB layers. Scale and colour bars apply to all panels. e, Sketch of the simulated multilayer stack.
Figure 4 shows cross-section snapshots of the relaxed states at representative fields. Starting from

Figure 5: Return-point memory experiments for the two skyrmion families. a, Four nucleation paths are shown on the positive branch of the hysteresis loop: conventional-polarity skyrmions nucleated from magnetic saturation (1) or a maze-domain state (2); and inverse-polarity skyrmions nucleated from the zero-field SAF state (3) or maze-domain state (4). b, For each path, the initial skyrmion map (repeat 0) was overlaid with successive skyrmion maps (repeats 1 to 9) to calculate the RPM. Exemplary skyrmion maps from path 1 in the inset show that repeat 1 gets overlaid on repeat 0. c, Cross-type RPM analysis, in which an initial skyrmion map (repeat 0) nucleated via one path was overlaid with...

Figure 6: Illustration of the effective-field mechanism generating two skyrmion families. Top-down (thickness-averaged) and cross-section views of the SAF through the sequence FM satura- tion →conventional-polarity skyrmions →maze-domains →inverse-polarity skyrmions →uniform SAF state. Grey indicates local antiferromagnetic alignment (zero net moment); red indicates locally ferromagnetic alignment. Skyrmion tubes form in CoFeB within a ferromagnetic background and reverse core polarity as the net effective field on CoFeB changes sign due to competi- tion between the external field (up) and the antiparallel RKKY field imposed by the CoB sublattice across the 0.7 nm Ru spacers.
